target.title: fix(doctor): merge colliding model-ref map keys instead of dropping
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw doctor --fix` upgrades retired model refs in config. For a `models` map (keyed by model ref, such as `agents.defaults.models` and per-agent `agents.list[].models`), `rewriteModelRefMapKeys` rewrites each key to its current ref. When two retired keys upgrade to the SAME current ref, the second entry is silently dropped with no merge and no warning.

`src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.models.ts` before:

```ts
if (nextKey in next && upgradedKey) {
  continue;
}
next[nextKey] = child;
```

Many retired ids collapse to one current ref. For example both `openai/gpt-4o` and `openai/gpt-4` upgrade to `openai/gpt-5.5`. A user who keeps distinct per-model tuning under two such keys (different `params`, `streaming`, `alias`, `agentRuntime`) loses one entry's entire config. Worse, the change log already pushed a success line for the dropped key (`Upgraded ... key from X to Y.`), so the migration report FALSELY claims the dropped key was upgraded.

## Why This Change Was Made

The sibling provider-models-array path in this same file (issue #90047, `migrateLegacyOpenAICodexProvider`) already merges disjoint entries on collision and warns when a safe merge is impossible, rather than dropping. The map-key path was the unguarded sibling. This change applies the same merge-or-warn semantics so a doctor migration never silently destroys user config.

`rewriteModelRefMapKeys` after, on a key collision:

```ts
if (Object.hasOwn(next, nextKey)) {
  const existing = next[nextKey];
  const canonicalWins = isCanonicalEntry && !canonicalKeys.has(nextKey);
  const { value, conflicts } = canonicalWins
    ? mergeModelRefMapEntries(child, existing)
    : mergeModelRefMapEntries(existing, child);
  setModelRefMapEntry(next, nextKey, value);
  const sourceKey = canonicalWins ? (retiredSourceKeys.get(nextKey) ?? key) : key;
  // ... change-log line naming sourceKey -> nextKey
  continue;
}
```

`mergeModelRefMapEntries` deep-merges the two entries: disjoint fields are preserved, nested records (`params`, `agentRuntime`) merge recursively, and when a scalar field is defined on both with different values the existing value is kept deterministically and the field is named in a collision change-log line. No entry is ever silently dropped.

Two correctness refinements over the first revision of this patch:

- Both sides of the merge are now prototype-key safe. Because the merge copies fields from one user config entry into another plain object, it shares the prototype-key hazard the rest of the config merge code guards against. The first revision filtered `isBlockedObjectKey` fields (`__proto__`, `prototype`, `constructor`) only on the incoming side, but it spread the existing entry wholesale (`{ ...existingRecord }`), so a blocked key carried by the FIRST colliding entry survived into the merged object and could mutate its prototype during the later rewrite. `mergeModelRefMapEntries` now filters blocked keys when copying the existing side too, matching the incoming side and the `isBlockedObjectKey` guard `applyMergePatch` uses in `src/config/merge-patch.ts`. A crafted colliding entry on either side can no longer pollute the merged entry's prototype.
- The merge change-log line now names the retired source key. When a retired key is processed before the canonical key it upgrades onto, the canonical entry's values win, but `key` at that point is the canonical ref itself, so the line read `Merged "openai/gpt-5.5" into "openai/gpt-5.5"`. The migration now tracks the retired source key per upgraded slot and reports it, so the line reads `Merged "openai/gpt-4" into "openai/gpt-5.5"` regardless of collision order.

## User Impact

After upgrading and running the standard `openclaw doctor --fix`, users who had two retired model-ref keys collapsing to one current ref keep both entries' tuning instead of losing one. The change log reports a truthful merge/collision line (naming the retired source key in either collision order) instead of a false success. A hostile or malformed config file with a `__proto__` field in a colliding model entry can no longer pollute prototypes during the merge, whether the field sits on the existing or the incoming side of the collision.

## Why this is the right boundary

This is doctor/migration code, the only place legacy config shapes are normalized, so the fix lives where ownership belongs. It reuses the existing `getRecord` and `hasOwnDefinedProperty` helpers, mirrors the already-merged sibling array path, and reuses the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` guard rather than inventing a new one. Existing single-key upgrades and the existing change-log success lines for non-colliding keys are unchanged. The other model-ref surfaces (string keys, array keys) cannot collide because they do not key a map.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: doctor --fix collapsed colliding model-map keys to one current ref and silently dropped one entry's entire tuning while logging a false upgrade-success line. This holds for two retired keys upgrading to the same ref AND for a retired key upgrading onto a key that already exists as a canonical (current, non-retired) entry; in the canonical case the retired entry's disjoint fields were also lost. The collision merge that replaces the drop keeps canonical values on conflict, must name the retired source key in either collision order, and must not let a colliding entry's `__proto__` field mutate the merged entry prototype from EITHER side of the merge.

Real environment tested: the real `applyLegacyDoctorMigrations` entry point in `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-compat.ts` (the migration runner doctor calls) driven on identical input on pristine main `6830aa39eaa16429daec9e03688e6e04e9e75841` and on PR head `ac1bd125ad6e28507eb6d65064b19e9bf229c1e0`; the real migration pipeline, real upgrade map, and real change-log all stayed real with no external seam mocked. The prototype-safety scenarios additionally compare the prior single-sided-guard head `ea385840f672cbbf20b2abdb4f799f7112d96fc0` (the revision a from-scratch review flagged) against this head to show the existing-side hole is closed.

Exact steps or command run: invoke `applyLegacyDoctorMigrations` with each input below and print the surviving map, its change-log lines, and (for the prototype scenario) the merged entry's own keys and prototype.

Evidence:
```
# 1. COLLISION MERGE (two retired keys -> same ref)
# input: agents.defaults.models = {
#   "openai/gpt-4o": { params:{reasoning_effort:"high"}, streaming:false },
#   "openai/gpt-4":  { params:{reasoning_effort:"low"},  alias:"legacy-four" } }
# BEFORE (pristine main 6830aa39eaa16429daec9e03688e6e04e9e75841)
surviving = { "openai/gpt-5.5": { "params": { "reasoning_effort": "high" }, "streaming": false } }
change-log:
  Upgraded config.agents.defaults.models key from "openai/gpt-4o" to "openai/gpt-5.5".
  Upgraded config.agents.defaults.models key from "openai/gpt-4" to "openai/gpt-5.5".
# AFTER (head ac1bd125ad6e28507eb6d65064b19e9bf229c1e0, identical input)
surviving = { "openai/gpt-5.5": { "params": { "reasoning_effort": "high" }, "streaming": false, "alias": "legacy-four" } }
change-log adds:
  Merged config.agents.defaults.models key "openai/gpt-4" into "openai/gpt-5.5"; kept existing values for conflicting fields: params.reasoning_effort.

# 2. CANONICAL ALREADY PRESENT, RETIRED UPGRADES ONTO IT
# input: { "openai/gpt-5.5": { params:{reasoning_effort:"medium"}, streaming:true },
#          "openai/gpt-4":  { params:{reasoning_effort:"low"},    alias:"legacy-four" } }
# BEFORE (pristine main): surviving openai/gpt-5.5 = { params:{reasoning_effort:"medium"}, streaming:true }  (alias lost, retired entry dropped)
# AFTER  (this head):     surviving openai/gpt-5.5 = { params:{reasoning_effort:"medium"}, streaming:true, alias:"legacy-four" }
change-log adds:
  Merged config.agents.defaults.models key "openai/gpt-4" into "openai/gpt-5.5"; kept existing values for conflicting fields: params.reasoning_effort.

# 3. RETIRED-SOURCE-KEY NAMING (retired key processed FIRST, canonical SECOND)
# input: { "openai/gpt-4": { params:{reasoning_effort:"low"}, alias:"legacy-four" },
#          "openai/gpt-5.5": { params:{reasoning_effort:"medium"}, streaming:true } }
# BEFORE (prior head ea385840f672cbbf20b2abdb4f799f7112d96fc0, single-sided revision)
change-log merge line:
  Merged config.agents.defaults.models key "openai/gpt-5.5" into "openai/gpt-5.5"; kept existing values for conflicting fields: params.reasoning_effort.   <- canonical key named against itself
# AFTER (head ac1bd125ad6e28507eb6d65064b19e9bf229c1e0, identical input)
surviving = { "openai/gpt-5.5": { "params": { "reasoning_effort": "medium" }, "streaming": true, "alias": "legacy-four" } }
change-log merge line:
  Merged config.agents.defaults.models key "openai/gpt-4" into "openai/gpt-5.5"; kept existing values for conflicting fields: params.reasoning_effort.   <- retired source key reported

# 4. PROTOTYPE SAFETY, BLOCKED KEY ON THE FIRST/EXISTING COLLIDING ENTRY
# input: { "openai/gpt-4o": { __proto__:{polluted:true}, fallback:["openai/gpt-4o"], streaming:false },
#          "openai/gpt-4":  { alias:"legacy-four" } }
# (the retired fallback forces the entry rewrite that realizes the prototype mutation)
# BEFORE (prior head ea385840f672cbbf20b2abdb4f799f7112d96fc0, existing side spread unguarded)
own keys of merged entry: ["fallback","streaming","alias"]
merged entry prototype is Object.prototype: false
merged.polluted: true
# AFTER (head ac1bd125ad6e28507eb6d65064b19e9bf229c1e0, identical input)
own keys of merged entry: ["fallback","streaming","alias"]
merged entry prototype is Object.prototype: true
merged.polluted: undefined
merged = { "fallback": ["openai/gpt-5.5"], "streaming": false, "alias": "legacy-four" }
# (a blocked key carried only as an own data key, with no retired field to force a rewrite, also no longer
#  survives into the merged entry: own keys = ["streaming","alias"], matching the incoming-side guarantee.)
```

Observed result after fix: colliding retired/retired and canonical-vs-retired entries merge instead of dropping (disjoint `alias`/`streaming` preserved, conflicting `params.reasoning_effort` resolves deterministically to the existing/canonical value); the merge change-log names the retired source key in BOTH collision orders instead of naming the canonical key against itself; and a colliding `__proto__` field on EITHER side of the merge no longer reaches the merged entry, so its prototype stays `Object.prototype` with no injected `polluted` member.

What was not tested: no live end-to-end CLI invocation of the full `openclaw doctor --fix` command; the migration function was driven directly with identical input. Full build not run.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`: 121 passed (the collision-merge tests, both prototype-safety tests, the new retired-source-key naming test, and the existing suite).
- New test `does not pollute prototypes when the first colliding entry carries a blocked key` fails on the prior single-sided-guard revision (merged entry prototype is mutated, `polluted` readable through the chain) and passes with the both-sides guard.
- New test `names the retired source key when the canonical key follows the retired key` fails on the prior revision (line names the canonical key against itself) and passes with the source-key tracking.
- The existing `merges colliding model ref map keys ...`, `merges when the canonical key follows the retired key`, `keeps canonical values when canonical and retired keys collide`, and `does not pollute prototypes when merging colliding model ref map keys` tests remain green.
- `oxlint <changed files>`: clean.
- `oxfmt --check <changed files>`: clean.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`: clean.
target.author: yetval
